coredata.dc:title: A facile one-pot synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from degrease cotton for the selective determination of chromium ions in water and soil samples
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Jie
Qiu, Fengxian
Li, Xin
Wu, Haiyan
Xu, Jicheng
Niu, Xiangheng
Pan, Jianming
Zhang, Tao
Yang, Dongya
coredata.dc:description: A highly fluorescent carbon dots were prepared by a facile one-pot hydrothermal method using degrease cotton (human waste) as the carbon source. The structure and morphology of prepared C-dots were characterized by fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The as-prepared C-dots exhibit excellent emission property, high stability and have a narrow size distribution of 2–4nm in diameter. Moreover, it is attractive that C-dots can be used as an effective fluorescent probe for the detection of chromium ions with good selectivity and sensitivity in an aqueous solution. The quenching phenomenon was observed after the C-dots interacted with the metal ions; and the results showed that the optimum conditions for its interaction with chromium ions were: 4.0×10−5 g/mL of concentration of C-dots, pH=6.5 of buffer solution, and 20min of time. Based on the optimum conditions, a new sensitive method detecting chromium ions was established. The method has been successfully applied to detect chromium ions in water and soil samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Degrease cotton
Carbon dots
Chromium ions
Fluorophotometry

Synthesis of fluorescent C-dots
The interaction between the fluorescent C-dots and Cr(VI) ions
Application of prepared FCDs-IPDI-CD for detection of Cr(VI) in soil
Results and discussion

TEM and FT-IR analysis
Optimization of preparation conditions of C-dots
The effects of the hydrothermal temperature on the fluorescent spectra of C-dots
The effects of the different hydrothermal time on the fluorescent spectra of C-dots
The effects of the amount of degrease cotton on the fluorescent spectra of C-dots
The effect on the fluorescence intensity of the as-prepared C-dots solution
The effect of the C-dots concentration on the fluorescence intensity
The effect of different pH on the fluorescent intensity
The effect of the different solvent on the fluorescence intensity
The effect of the preservation time on the fluorescence intensity
The effect of the ionic strength on the fluorescent intensity
The interaction between the C-dots and Cr(VI)
Optimization of the conditions for determining Cr(VI)
Standard linear curve and selectivity of prepared C-dots
Application of prepared C-dots for detection of Cr(VI) in water and soil samples
Possible mechanism of C-dots to Cr(VI)
Conclusions
Acknowledgements
References

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of C-dots (a) and emission spectra of the C-dots with excitation of different wavelength (b).
TEM image (a) and particle size distribution (b) of as prepared C-dots.
FT-IR spectra of degrease cotton (a) and C-dots (b).
The effects of the hydrothermal temperature (a), hydrothermal time (b) and amount of degrease cotton (c) on the fluorescent spectra of C-dots.
The effects of C-dots concentration (a), different pH (b), different solvent (c), preservation time (d) and ionic strength (e) on the fluorescent intensity.
Comparative spectra of C-dots and C-dots + Cr(VI) ions (a); effects of C-dots concentration (b), reaction time (c) and different pH (d) on relative fluorescence intensity.
The standard linear curve of the reaction system (a) and selectivity of Cr(VI) using C-dots as probes (b).
Synthesis of C-dots from degrease cotton and detection of Cr(VI) ions with C-dots.

head.ce:title: A facile one-pot synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from degrease cotton for the selective determination of chromium ions in water and soil samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, 212013 Zhenjiang, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A highly fluorescent carbon dots were prepared by a facile one-pot hydrothermal method using degrease cotton (human waste) as the carbon source. The structure and morphology of prepared C-dots were characterized by fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The as-prepared C-dots exhibit excellent emission property, high stability and have a narrow size distribution of 2–4nm in diameter. Moreover, it is attractive that C-dots can be used as an effective fluorescent probe for the detection of chromium ions with good selectivity and sensitivity in an aqueous solution. The quenching phenomenon was observed after the C-dots interacted with the metal ions; and the results showed that the optimum conditions for its interaction with chromium ions were: 4.0×10−5 g/mL of concentration of C-dots, pH=6.5 of buffer solution, and 20min of time. Based on the optimum conditions, a new sensitive method detecting chromium ions was established. The method has been successfully applied to detect chromium ions in water and soil samples with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, fluorescent nanoparticles have drawn extensive attention due to their attractive properties and their different kinds of applications in biology, chemistry and so on [1,2]. Fluorescent semiconductor nanomaterials, usually named quantum dots (QDs), have comprehensive application in a lot of fields, for example, biomedical imaging and sensing [3,4], photovoltaic devices [5,6], and lasers [7]. In spite of the particular characters and outstanding performance of semiconductor quantum dots, they have obvious disadvantages. Most of the high-performance QDs are composed of toxic heavy metal element such as cadmium, which limit their use.

Carbon dots (C-dots), as a new fluorescence carbon nanomaterial, have got extensive attention owning to their easy preparation, small size effects, low cost, low toxicity, wonderful biocompatibility, and good photostability and without cytotoxicity. Nanoparticles make up an attractive kind of recently discovered nanocarbons that are composed of discrete, quasi spherical nanoparticles with sizes below 10nm [8–10]. Moreover, fluorescent C-dots with stable emission are promising replacements to well developed fluorescent semiconductor nanocrystals, that constitute toxic heavy metals such as cadmium and lead ions during the application process [11,12].

Nowadays a lot of methods for the synthesis of C-dots have been reported, for example, laser ablation, electrochemical oxidation, ultrasonic treatment and microwave methods [13–15]. However, the majority of these ways are dissatisfied because of low fluorescence quantum output, expensive equipment or complex processes. Therefore, there is a good need to develop simple, low-cost and high-yield way for the synthesis of C-dots with strong fluorescence emission. Moreover, application of fluorescent nanomaterials as a probe is the current tendency. The application of C-dots which determine the metal ions selectively is in the stage of development. The environmental and health harms that are caused by the existence of poisonous chemical waste including heavy metals attract a great attention in the industrial world.

Chromium is the one of the most commonly existed metal elements in the crust and the content of it is more abundant than many other common metal elements. Agricultural soils are easy to pollute by chromium that mainly comes from stainless steel welding, steelmaking, cement plant, electroplating, spinning and so on [16]. Because the chemical property of chromium is relatively stable, oxidation-reduction reaction does not happen. And in the nature, most chromium nearly does not dissolve in the water. Chromium and its compounds are the basic raw materials in the industries of metallurgy, metal processing, electroplating, and leather and so on. However, during the production process, the discharge of waste water, waste residue and waste gas containing chromium ions leads to contamination of the soil. Cr(VI) has become an indispensable element in environmental monitoring. And the ways of determining Cr(VI) get the extensive attention. Recently, a lot of ways for determining metal ions, including optical methods [17], electrochemical methods [18] and atomic absorption spectrometry [19] have been developed; and they also can provide satisfactory results. In this work, the fluorescent C-dots were prepared by hydrothermal method using degrease cotton (human waste) as the carbon source. This method does not passivate the C-dots. Moreover, the advantages are mainly as follows: the carbon source is abundant; the fabrication process is simple and environmentally-friendly. And chromium in soil was determined by fluorospectrophotometry using fluorescent C-dots as a fluorescent probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental
Apparatus and reagents
ce:section.ce:para: The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were carried out using a JEM-2100 Field Emission Transmission electron microscope (Japan). All fluorescence spectra were obtained on a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Australia Varian co., LTD), which can characterize the optical property of C-dots including excitation wavelength, emission wavelength and fluorescence intensity. UV–vis absorption spectra measurements were carried out using UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum in the 4000–1000cm−1 region was recorded using a Nicolet Nexus 470 FT-IR spectroscope (Nicolet Instrument Co., USA).

Acetone, ethyl alcohol, methyl alcohol, petroleum ether were analytically pure reagents and degrease cotton was used without further purification. All the others regents were of analytical grade and ultrapure water were used throughout the experiment. The pH of C-dots solution was adjusted by HAc-NaAc.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of fluorescent C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent C-dots were prepared by a facile one-pot hydrothermal method using degrease cotton (human waste) as the carbon source. 1.0g of degrease cotton was dissolved in 70mL distilled water to form a clear solution. Then the solution was transferred into a 100mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200°C for 13h. After the reaction was completed, the autoclave was cooled down naturally. Next, the solution was subjected to suction filtration to separate the filtrate from the residue. And then the aqueous solution was centrifuged at 8000rpm for 20min to dislodge the non-fluorescent deposition. Finally, the purified fluorescent C-dots solution was obtained and stored at 4°C in the refrigerator. The fluorescent C-dots powder was obtained by evaporating the C-dots solution for standby.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The interaction between the fluorescent C-dots and Cr(VI) ions
ce:section.ce:para: HAc-NaAc buffer solution, 10mL C-dots solution (above optimum conditions) and 10mL Cr(VI) (100mg/L, K2Cr2O7) standard solution were added into the colorimetric tube and mixed. Then, the aqueous solution was shaken by the ultrasonic dispersion at 99 frequency of sonication. The fluorescence spectra was determined on a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Australia Varian Co., LTD), using a quartz cuvette and the excitation and emission slit widths were 5nm (excitation wavelength, 355nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of prepared FCDs-IPDI-CD for detection of Cr(VI) in soil
ce:section.ce:para: The soil was dried and was crushed by a wooden stick. Then the soil was passed through the nylon sieve with an average pore diameter of 2mm. The sifted samples were separated over and over by quartering. And 250g above samples were finely grinded in an agate mortar to make them go through the 160 mesh nylon sieve and mixed well. 0.5g (accurate to 0.0001g) air-dried soil sample was accurately weighed and transferred to the Teflon crucible; after dampening the sample using water, 5mL concentrated nitric acid (HNO3) was added. The crucible was sealed and heated at 100–150°C for 20min and then cooled down. 5mL perchloric acid (HClO4) was added and heated at 200°C for 10min. And, 5mL hydrofluoric acid (HF) was added and heated at 80°C for 60min. Then the temperature gradually rose to evaporate HF and when the white smoke occurred, the sample was cooled down. Then 5mL of HF and 2mL of HClO4 was added in it and were evaporated at about 250°C to be near dry and was taken down to cool. Adding 4mL hydrochloric acid solution (1: 1) and a little water, the residue was heated to dissolve and was transferred to a 50mL volumetric flask. Then 10.00mL solution was transferred to another 100mL volumetric flask, and determination solution of the soil was obtained. The blank solution was also prepared by the above method. 2mL soil sample solution was transferred to a 50mL volumetric flask and then the content of chromium in soil was determined by the experimental method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra
ce:section.ce:para: UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of C-dots are shown in Fig. 1(a). As shown in Fig. 1(a), C-dots solution has an absorption in the ultraviolet region and then gradually decline to the visible region. Several humps over the wavelength range between 250 and 700nm are observed. The absorption peak at 270nm can be ascribed to π–π* electronic transition of C=OCO groups. The peak at 305nm can be attributed to the amide-containing fluorophores, which are formed in the dehydration process. The peak centered about 350nm due to the trapping of excited state energy by the surface states leads to emission [12]. The absorption peak centered at 410nm corresponds to carbonyl functional groups on the surface surface/molecular center [20]. From Fig. 1(a), the maximum excitation wavelength and maximum emission wavelength of the C-dots are at 355 and 440nm, respectively. As can be seen from Fig. 1(b), the emission peaks of C-dots at various excitation wavelengths did not shift, and the maximum emission wavelength at different excitation wavelengths remained at 440nm. It was attributed to the high size uniformity and the homogeneous surface state of sp2 clusters. The property of excitation-independent emissions is much different from most of the previous reported C-dots [21,22], which can avoid auto fluorescence during their applications. When the excitation wavelength is 355nm, the fluorescence intensity value reached its maximum. Therefore, 355nm as the excitation wavelength was selected for the following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TEM and FT-IR analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was operated on a Philips TECNAI-12 with an acceleration voltage of 120kV. The size and morphology of the as-prepared C-dots was observed by TEM. Fig. 2(a) shows a TEM image of the C-dots obtained by the hydrothermal treatment at 200°C and Fig. 2(b) shows their size distribution. TEM image clearly reveals that the product is homogeneously dispersed C-dots with a spherical morphology and a narrow size distribution of 2–4nm in diameter. These fluorescent C-dots can be freely dispersed in water and are called the "water-soluble fluorescent C-dots".

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy was used for identification of the functional groups in the C-dots. FT-IR spectra of degrease cotton (a) and C-dots (b) are shown in Fig. 3. As shown in Fig. 3(b), the C-dots exhibit a main characteristic absorption band of O-H stretching vibration mode at 3407cm−1 [23], while a C-H stretching vibration band appears at 2935cm−1. The band at 1624cm−1 corresponds to C=C stretching of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons. The vibration band at 1710cm−1 is attributed to the carbonyl (C=O) groups. The peaks from 1390cm−1 to 1000cm−1 correspond to the symmetric stretching vibrations of –C-OH [24] and the bending vibration of -OH, respectively, which show that there are a large number of the remaining hydroxyls. The result shows that there are rich in oxygen-containing groups (O-H and carbonyl (C=O) groups) in the surface of the fluorescent C-dots, improving the hydrophilic and stability of the fluorescent C-dots in aqueous systems. These results show that degrease cotton after the hydrothermal reaction, could be changed into the fluorescent C-dots with the sectional functional surface. Moreover, the FTIR spectrum of C-dots has a new peak at 1710cm−1 and the peak intensity is very strong. The intensity of peak at 1624cm−1 is weaker than the degrease cotton, indicating that the structure of degrease cotton has changed and broken down.

The quantum yield (QY) of the C-dots was determined at excitation wavelengths of 345nm and 365nm by the following Equation:
Q
x
=
Q
R
⋅
I
x
I
R
⋅
A
R
A
x
⋅
η
x
2
η
R
2
where ‘Q′ is the quantum yield. ‘I′ is the fluorescence intensity. ‘A′ is the absorbance at excitation wavelength and ‘g′ is the refractive index of the solvent used; using quinine sulfate (quantum yield 54) in 0.1molL−1 H2SO4 solution as the reference. The subscripts ‘x′ for sample and ‘R′ for reference are used in this equation. The quantum yields are 10.2%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of preparation conditions of C-dots
The effects of the hydrothermal temperature on the fluorescent spectra of C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: Fixed the amount of degrease cotton of 1.0g, distilled water of 70mL and hydrothermal time of 12h, the effects of the hydrothermal temperature on the fluorescent spectra of C-dots are shown in Fig. 4(a). As can be seen from Fig. 4(a), with the increasing temperature from 180°C to 220°C, the fluorescence intensity first increases and then decreases. However, the emission wavelength almost did not change along temperature. When temperature is 200°C, the fluorescence intensity value reached its maximum. This is mainly because the reaction is more and more sufficient with increasing temperature and the intensity of solution increases. But at higher temperature, the surface structure of C-dots will be destroyed; and the fluorescence intensity could be reduced. Therefore, 200°C was chosen for best hydrothermal temperature in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effects of the different hydrothermal time on the fluorescent spectra of C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: Fixed the amount of degrease cotton of 1.0g, distilled water of 70mL and hydrothermal temperature of 200°C, the effects of the hydrothermal time on the fluorescent spectra of C-dots are shown in Fig. 4(b). As can be seen from Fig. 4(b), as hydrothermal time from 12h to 17h, the fluorescence intensity first increases and then decreases. When the hydrothermal time is 15h, the fluorescence intensity value reached its maximum. The reason is probably that if the hydrothermal time is too long, the surface structure of C-dots will be destroyed, and the system can reduce the fluorescence intensity. However, the emission wavelength nearly did not change with the increasing time. As a result, 15h was selected as the optimum hydrothermal time in this work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effects of the amount of degrease cotton on the fluorescent spectra of C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4(c) shows the effects of the amount of degrease cotton on the fluorescent spectra of C-dots at the fixed the distilled water of 70mL, hydrothermal temperature of 200°C and hydrothermal time of 15h. As seen from Fig. 4(c), along with the amount of degrease cotton increases from 0.5g to 1.8g, the fluorescence intensity increases at first and decreases afterwards. When the degrease cotton dosage is 1.2g, the fluorescence intensity value reached its maximum. At the same time, the emission wavelength nearly did not change with the increasing dosage. The possible reason for this phenomenon is that the amount of fluorescent C-dots increases with the increase of the amount of degreases cotton. However, when the amount of degreases cotton increases to a certain value, the reaction space in the limited reactor kettle decreases that leads to the insufficiency of reaction, making the decrease of the fluorescence intensity. As a result, 1.2g degrease cotton into 70mL distilled water was chosen as the optimum dosage in this work.

In conclusion, the optimum preparation conditions of the C-dots are as follows: the dosage of the degrease cotton is 1.2g of degrease cotton into 70mL, hydrothermal temperature is 200°C and hydrothermal time is 15h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect on the fluorescence intensity of the as-prepared C-dots solution
The effect of the C-dots concentration on the fluorescence intensity
ce:section.ce:para: The stock C-dots solution (1.0×10−3g/mL) was diluted to different multiples using water to get the different fluorescence spectra (above optimum conditions). The above text was carrying out when the C-dots solution is diluted to 10 times (1.0×10−4 g/mL). As can be seen from Fig. 5(a), the fluorescence intensity first increases and decreases afterwards at the different C-dots concentration from 10 times to 200 times and the emission wavelength nearly did not change, indicating that the C-dots can be dispersed stably in aqueous solution. At the same time, the Fig. 5(a) shows that when the C-dots solution is diluted to 50 times, the fluorescence intensity value reached its maximum. The obtained result is probably because the concentration of C-dots solution is too large and then the distances of C-dots decreases, that make the C-dots appear the coagulation or agglomeration. As a result, the quenching phenomenon took place and the fluorescence intensity decreases. However, when the concentration is too low, the amount of C-dots in the unit volume solution decreases, making the fluorescence intensity decrease. So, stock C-dots solution (1.0×10−3g/mL) was diluted to 50 times as the optimum concentration of C-dots in this work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of different pH on the fluorescent intensity
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5(b) shows the effect of different pH on the fluorescence intensity and the experiment conducted at the same excitation wavelength and different pH. The C-dots solution (1.0×10−4g/mL, pH=6.5) was diluted to different pH. As shown in Fig. 5(b), at lower pH, C-dots solution shows small fluorescence intensity. At higher pH, C-dots solution has higher fluorescence intensity. When pH is above 8, the fluorescence intensity of C-dots solution decreases gradually and when the pH is 8, the fluorescence intensity value reached its maximum. The results may be attributed to a lot of hydroxyl groups in the surface of C-dots. These hydroxyl groups may be protonated in acidic solutions, leading to the aggregation of C-dots and the fluorescence quenching occurred. In the alkaline condition, deprotonation of chemical bonds occurs, making the carboxyl groups (-COOH) and the hydroxyl groups (-OH) get the negative charge. Then the C-dots is in a monodisperse condition because of the electrostatic repulsion. Therefore, pH=8 was selected as the optimum pH in this work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of the different solvent on the fluorescence intensity
ce:section.ce:para: The different kinds of solvent have different effect on the intensity of C-dots. In this work, water, acetone, ethyl alcohol, methyl alcohol and petroleum ether on the fluorescence intensity were investigated, and the result is shown in Fig. 5(c). As can be seen from Fig. 5(c), the fluorescence intensity value reached its maximum in the aqueous solution and it is weak in other solvents. The results can be attributed to the presence of a large number of hydroxyl groups and carboxyl groups on the surface of C-dots surface, according to the infrared spectrum of C-dots. So, the compatibility is better in the aqueous solution and it is difficult to dissolve in the strongly polar organic solvents. As a result, water was selected as the optimum solvent in this work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of the preservation time on the fluorescence intensity
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the fluorescent stability of the prepared C-dots by hydrothermal method, the C-dots solution was stored for one day, 15 days, 30 days, 45 days, 60 days, 120 days and 150 days, respectively. The effect of the preservation time on the fluorescence intensity is shown in Fig. 5(d). As can be seen from Fig. 5(d), there is a steady decrease in fluorescence intensity upon storage. However, the emission wavelength of C-dots almost did not change with the longer days and the fluorescence intensity changed little. The result indicates that the fluorescence of C-dots solution is almost stable and can be stored for a long time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of the ionic strength on the fluorescent intensity
ce:section.ce:para: The concentrations of NaCl ranged from 0M to 2.0M. The effect of the ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity is shown in Fig. 5(e). In Fig. 5(e), the fluorescence intensity of C-dots nearly did not change with ionic strength, indicating that C-dots have greater salt resistance. As a result, the C-dots can be applied to the fields of biochemical assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The interaction between the C-dots and Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para:
The interactions between the fluorescent C-dots and metal ions are shown in Fig. 6(a). As can be seen from Fig. 6(a), fluorescence quenching phenomenon was observed. The reason is mainly about the electrostatic interaction between the Cr(VI) ions and the negatively charged C-dots. The relative fluorescence intensity I
0
/ I can be obtained, where I
0 and I are the fluorescence intensities before and after the addition of Cr(VI), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the conditions for determining Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental conditions were above optimum and other conditions were invariant. Then different C-dots concentrations (0.2×10−5 g/mL, 0.5×10−5 g/mL, 1.0×10−5 g/mL, 2.0×10−5 g/mL, 4.0×10−5 g/mL, 6.0×10−5 g/mL, 8.0×10−5 g/mL and 10.0×10−5 g/mL) were added and mixed with HAc-NaAc buffer solution and 10mL Cr(VI) standard solution. The effect of C-dots concentration on relative fluorescence intensity is shown in Fig. 6(b). From Fig. 6(b), the relative fluorescence intensity first increases and decreases afterwards at the different C-dots concentration from 0.2×10−5 g/mL to 10.0×10−5 g/mL. When the C-dots concentration is 4.0×10−5 g/mL, the relative fluorescence intensity value reached its maximum. As a result, 4.0×10−5 g/mL, the concentration of C-dots is selected in this work.

As can be seen from Fig. 6(c), the effect of reaction time between the C-dots and chromium ions on the relative fluorescence intensity can be known. After adding the 10mL Cr(VI) standard solution, the relative fluorescence intensity was stabilized after 20min and changed little after a few hours. When the above solution was stored for several days, the fluorescence also can be detected. This result indicates that the system for detecting Cr(VI) by C-dots is stable. Therefore, for the hydrothermal method, the optimal reaction time is just 20min to determine Cr(VI) by C-dots.

The experiment adopted HAc-NaAc buffer solution to adjust the acidity of the solution. The effect of different pH on relative fluorescence intensity is shown in Fig. 6(d). From Fig. 6(d), the relative fluorescence intensity first increases and then decreases at the different pH from 1.5 to 9.5. And when the pH was 6.5, the relative fluorescence intensity value reached its maximum. The reason is mainly attributed to the enhancement of the hydrolysis for metal ions and the concentration of metal ions decreases. Then fluorescence quenching ability weakened gradually. As a result, the pH value of 6.5 is selected as the optimal acidity.
In conclusion, the optimum conditions of detecting Cr(VI) are as follows: the concentration of C-dots is 4.0×10−5 g/mL, time is 20min and pH is 6.5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Standard linear curve and selectivity of prepared C-dots
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence of C-dots could be quenched by the Cr(VI). The analytical performance of the detection system for quantitative detection of Cr(VI) under the optimized conditions was evaluated. The relative fluorescence intensity versus the concentration of Cr(VI) is shown in Fig. 7(a). The linear regression equation is I
0 /I =0.4577C +5.8613, where I
0 and I are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of Cr(VI), respectively. A good linear correlation (R2 =0.9979) is observed over the concentration range of 1.00–6.00mmol/L. The C-dots would aggregate with each other in the presence of Cr(VI), resulting in the fluorescence quenching of C-dots.

To evaluate the selectivity of the detection of Cr(VI), the fluorescence changes of the C-dots in the presence of other representative metal ions (100mg/L) under the same conditions were examined. As shown in Fig. 7(b), the result demonstrates that common metal ions (Fe3+, K+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Mg2+ and Na+) have little effects on the relative fluorescence intensity quenching. The effect of Cr(VI) on relative fluorescence intensity quenching is the most obvious. Therefore, these results directly illustrate that the prepared C-dots can be developed as a rapid response fluorescent probe for detection of Cr(VI) with a high sensitivity and selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of prepared C-dots for detection of Cr(VI) in water and soil samples
ce:section.ce:para: The recovery of water sample experiment was studied by fluorescence spectrometry in the homemade water samples that contained chromium ions. According to the above method, three kinds of different concentrations of water samples were prepared that included chromium ions and the content of chromium ions can be detected and the results are listed in Table 1. The recovery of water sample ranged from 96.70% to 99.20% with satisfactory results; and the relative standard deviation (RSD) is ranged from 0.20% to 0.62%. As can be seen from Table 1, the result indicates that traces of chromium ions can be detected by this fluorescence system of C-dots/buffer solution (pH =6.5) in the aqueous solution. The limit of detection (LOD) was found 0.12μg/mL.

As can be seen from Table 2, the proposed method has been successfully applied to the determination of chromium ions in soil samples with satisfactory result and recoveries ranged from 94.15% to 97.50%. The RSD is ranged from 0.59% to 0.85% and the LOD was found 0.19μg/mL. In this work, the method is simple and the sensitivity is excellent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of C-dots to Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: The C-dots with good water-solubility and high stability can still exhibit strong fluorescence intensity even at very low concentration. According to the above results, the good photostability of C-dots guarantees the stability of the fluorescence signal, ensuring the accuracy of the detection results. The as-prepared C-dots have strong emission and excellent stability, and the fluorescence emission is independent on excitation wavelengths. The C-dots show excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence with the maximum emission and excitation at 440 and 355nm, respectively. It has been found that the C-dots can be used to detect Cr(VI). The nature can provide a variety of carbon sources that can be directly prepared into C-dots. The experiment has recently proposed a hydrothermal method for the rapid synthesis of C-dots using degrease cotton as carbon source. As seen from Fig. 8, the fluorescent C-dots exhibit excellent fluorescence properties and can be used to detect Cr(VI). The excellent result is due to the strong interaction between Cr(VI) and the oxygen-containing groups on C-dots surface, which results in fluorescence quenching via electron, energy transfer or fluorescent inner filter effect [24,25]. The high selectivity is attributed to the fact that Cr(VI) has a stronger affinity toward the electron-rich surface of C-dots than other selected low valence metal ions (Fe3+, K+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Mg2+ and Na+). As a result, the C-dots can be used as a probe for selectively sensing Cr(VI). The feasibility of C-dots is also demonstrated with soil samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent C-dots were prepared by hydrothermal method using degrease cotton. The structure and morphology of C-dots were characterized by fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The conditions for preparing the C-dots were optimized. When the C-dots interacted with the metal chromium ions, the fluorescence quenching phenomenon occurred. The effects of environment factors (such as concentration of C-dots, reaction time, pH) on relative fluorescence intensity were investigated. Based on the optimal conditions, a new sensitive method for determining chromium ions was established; and the linear dynamic ranged from 1 to 6mmol/L with the correlation coefficient of 0.9979. The method has been successfully applied to detect chromium ions in water with satisfactory result; recovery ranged from 96.7% to 99.2%. The recovery of sample was 94.15~97.50% in soil and the relative standard deviation was 0.59~0.85%.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by the Innovation Program for Graduate Education of Jiangsu Province (KYLX15_1041), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U1507115 and 31601549), the Natural Science of Jiangsu Province (BK20161362, 20161264 and 20160489), the Natural Science of Jiangsu Education (16KJB150045 and 16KJB150009) and 333 High-Level Personnel Training Project of Jiangsu Province (BRA2016142).
